file_name: Nie et al. - 2024 - Multi-path segmentation network based on CNN and transformer for skin lesion image.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes MSNet, a multi-path segmentation network combining CNN and Transformer architectures, to perform skin lesion segmentation on public datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018). It uses deep learning models (MSNet-1 and MSNet-2) with attention mechanisms to extract features and segment skin lesions for computer-aided diagnosis of skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new multi-path segmentation network (MSNet) combining CNN and Transformer architectures. It details the design of specific modules (ERM, attention mechanisms), describes implementation details (PyTorch, RTX 3090 Ti), and reports extensive quantitative and qualitative results from experiments on public datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, TN3K). It is published as a conference paper at ACCV 2024.

title: Multi-path Segmentation Network Based on CNN and Transformer for Skin Lesion Image
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion segmentation for computer-aided skin cancer diagnosis by proposing MSNet, a multi-path segmentation network that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Transformers. The architecture integrates an Efficient Residual Module (ERM) for local feature extraction with Transformer-based global information paths, enhanced by pixel and coordinate attention mechanisms. Two variants were developed: MSNet-1 for lightweight, real-time applications and MSNet-2 for high-accuracy requirements. The models were evaluated on three public skin lesion datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018) and the TN3K thyroid nodule dataset for generalization. MSNet-2 achieved state-of-the-art performance with mIoU scores of 91.22%, 84.63%, and 84.14% on the respective skin lesion datasets, while MSNet-1 maintained a balance of accuracy and inference speed at 99 FPS. The results demonstrate that MSNet offers excellent segmentation performance, generalization ability, and applicability across different clinical scenarios.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the segmentation of skin lesions in general, using public datasets (ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018) provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The task is binary segmentation (lesion vs. background) rather than multi-class classification of specific histopathological types.
Mentioned in the Introduction as the most malignant skin cancer to provide clinical context for the importance of early diagnosis, but not a specific target class for the segmentation model.
Used in the generalization experiment on the TN3K dataset to assess the model's ability to segment non-skin lesions, demonstrating cross-domain applicability.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise binary mask of skin lesions and thyroid nodules
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes MSNet for 'skin lesion segmentation' and 'thyroid nodule segmentation'. The model outputs pixel-level predictions (segmentation masks) evaluated using mIoU and Accuracy metrics on datasets ISIC2016, ISIC2017, ISIC2018, and TN3K. The task is binary segmentation (lesion vs. background).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (mIoU, Accuracy, FPS) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC2016.notes: MSNet-2 achieved the highest mIoU (91.22%) and Accuracy (97.27%) on this dataset.

ISIC2017.notes: MSNet-2 achieved the highest mIoU (84.63%) and Accuracy (93.19%) on this dataset.

ISIC2018.notes: MSNet-2 achieved the highest mIoU (84.14%) and Accuracy (92.88%). MSNet-1 maintained a speed of 99 FPS.

TN3K.notes: Used for generalization experiments on thyroid nodule segmentation. MSNet-2 achieved the highest mIoU (86.62%) and Accuracy (96.62%).

architecture_details.model_family: Multi-path segmentation network combining CNN and Transformer
attention_mechanisms: Pixel Attention (PA) at the front of the network
Coordinate Attention (CDA) at the end of the network
architecture_details.erm_configuration: 7 ERMs used for both MSNet-1 and MSNet-2

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a resolution of 512 × 256
conclusions: The proposed Multi-path Segmentation Network (MSNet), which combines CNN and Transformer architectures, achieves state-of-the-art segmentation performance on skin lesion datasets.
MSNet-2 achieved the highest accuracy with mIoU scores of 91.22%, 84.63%, and 84.14% on the ISIC2016, ISIC2017, and ISIC2018 datasets, respectively.
MSNet-1 provides a balance between accuracy and inference speed, achieving 99 FPS on the ISIC2018 dataset, making it suitable for real-time applications.

The Efficient Residual Module (ERM) effectively integrates multi-level features while maintaining a lightweight structure with a small number of parameters (0.911 M for MSNet-1 and 1.735 M for MSNet-2).
The integration of pixel attention and coordinate attention mechanisms enhances the network's perceptual ability and improves segmentation prediction accuracy.
The model demonstrates excellent generalization ability, as evidenced by high performance on the TN3K thyroid nodule dataset (mIoU of 86.62% for MSNet-2).
Using 7 Efficient Residual Modules (ERMs) was identified as the optimal configuration, balancing real-time requirements and accuracy for MSNet-1 and maximizing accuracy for MSNet-2.

The multi-path structure (Detail Information Path, Global Information Path, and Base Information Path) effectively captures both local details and global context, addressing the limitations of CNNs in modeling long-range dependencies.
important_remarks: The study lacks formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals); performance claims are based solely on absolute metric comparisons without statistical validation.
The model was trained entirely from scratch without pre-training, which may limit the generalizability of the learned features compared to models initialized with large-scale pre-trained weights.

No data augmentation techniques were employed during training, relying only on resizing images to 512x256, which may increase the risk of overfitting given the limited dataset sizes (e.g., 900 training images for ISIC2016).

The generalization experiment on the TN3K thyroid nodule dataset demonstrates cross-domain applicability but does not validate performance on other dermatological conditions or diverse skin types, limiting claims of broad clinical relevance.

The paper does not report any external validation on independent datasets outside of the provided ISIC and TN3K collections, raising concerns about potential dataset bias inherent to these specific public benchmarks.
Inference speed (FPS) was measured on a high-end NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti GPU; performance on edge devices or lower-power hardware suitable for real-time clinical deployment is not reported.
